title: terraform -> OpenTofu feature/functionality parity matrix (machine-readable appendix)

units.summary: The legacy terraform/.gitignore is a single-line file with one functional element: the ignore pattern `tfplan`. It contains no Terraform/OpenTofu HCL functionality (no resources, data sources, modules, variables, outputs, providers, locals, lifecycle blocks, meta-args, provisioners, cloud-init, DNS, or ansible wiring). The `tfplan` pattern is carried into the target tree's root .gitignore (line 6), which also broadens coverage with `*.tfplan` and `*.tfplan.*` (lines 7-8) plus many additional ignore rules (state files, tfvars, .terraform/, ansible, python, OS, IDE, Claude settings). No parity gap.

elements.name: tfplan
elements.detail: The legacy file is a single-line .gitignore whose only functional element is the ignore pattern `tfplan` (ignores Terraform/OpenTofu plan output files named exactly `tfplan`). It defines no Terraform resources, variables, outputs, providers, locals, or any HCL constructs — it is purely a VCS ignore rule.
elements.evidence: Source: /tmp/legacy-tf-audit/terraform/.gitignore line 1 = `tfplan` (only line). Target: "/Users/fcx/Developer/Personal/repos/FusionCloudX-Infrastructure/.gitignore":6 = `tfplan` (verified via grep -n). Target root .gitignore additionally broadens coverage with lines 7-8 (`*.tfplan`, `*.tfplan.*`).

units.summary: The legacy terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl is OpenTofu's auto-maintained provider dependency lock. It contains no infrastructure-defining constructs — its functional payload is 5 provider lock entries (version + constraints + hashes) plus a self-documenting header. All 5 providers (1password/onepassword 3.3.1, ansible/ansible 1.3.0, bpg/proxmox 0.107.0 exact-pinned, hashicorp/tls 4.3.0, and the custom-patched private-registry unifi 0.42.0-fcx1) are present at IDENTICAL versions/constraints in the tofu tree's 3 split lock files (compute/network/opconnect), verified by grep. Classified 'refactored' because the single legacy lock was split into 3 per-state locks and onepassword/tls were relocated to specific states per the documented state-split + opconnect Option D changes. Zero parity gaps: no MISSING, no unjustified drops. The tofu lock is a strict superset — it additionally pins hashicorp/null 3.3.0 in opconnect for the new op-CLI SSH-key write-back.

elements.name: Auto-maintained lock file header comment
elements.detail: Lines 1-2: '# This file is maintained automatically by "tofu init". # Manual edits may be lost in future updates.' Not functional config — boilerplate emitted by the lock mechanism itself.
elements.evidence: Identical auto-generated header is present in all 3 tofu lock files (tofu/compute/.terraform.lock.hcl, tofu/network/.terraform.lock.hcl, tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl). This is a lock-mechanism artifact, not hand-authored functionality; it regenerates on `tofu init`.

elements.name: 1password/onepassword provider lock (v3.3.1, constraint ~> 3.0)
elements.detail: Lines 4-24: registry.opentofu.org/1password/onepassword, version 3.3.1, constraints '~> 3.0', 8 zh: + 7 h1: hashes. In legacy, this provider drove 1Password item/SSH-key management.
elements.evidence: Lock entry present and version-identical in tofu/compute/.terraform.lock.hcl (provider "registry.opentofu.org/1password/onepassword" version=3.3.1 constraints '~> 3.0', confirmed via grep). The onepassword PROVIDER survives in the COMPUTE state only — per known intentional change 'opconnect Option D dropped the onepassword PROVIDER from opconnect' but '1Password ITEMS must still be created in compute (tofu/compute/secrets.tf)'. So the lock moved from the single legacy state into compute, matching the state split. Functionally equivalent provider pin, just relocated.

elements.name: ansible/ansible provider lock (v1.3.0, constraint ~> 1.3.0)
elements.detail: Lines 26-60: registry.opentofu.org/ansible/ansible, version 1.3.0, constraints '~> 1.3.0', 15 zh: + 14 h1: hashes. Drives ansible_host inventory resources.
elements.evidence: Lock entry present and version-identical in tofu/compute/.terraform.lock.hcl AND tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl (both: ansible/ansible version=1.3.0 constraints '~> 1.3.0', confirmed via grep). Provider pin carried into the two states that emit ansible inventory (compute has ansible-inventory.tf; opconnect locks it too). Same version/constraint, redistributed per the 3-state split.

elements.name: bpg/proxmox provider lock (v0.107.0, constraint 0.107.0 exact-pinned)
elements.detail: Lines 62-94: registry.opentofu.org/bpg/proxmox, version 0.107.0, constraints '0.107.0' (exact pin), 14 zh: + 13 h1: hashes. Core provider for all Proxmox VM/LXC resources.
elements.evidence: Lock entry present and version-identical in ALL 3 tofu lock files (compute, network, opconnect: bpg/proxmox version=0.107.0 constraints '0.107.0', confirmed via grep). The primary compute provider is pinned in every split state since every state manages Proxmox resources. Exact version pin preserved.

elements.name: hashicorp/tls provider lock (v4.3.0, constraint ~> 4.0)
elements.detail: Lines 96-131: registry.opentofu.org/hashicorp/tls, version 4.3.0, constraints '~> 4.0', 15 zh: + 15 h1: hashes. Drives tls_private_key generation.
elements.evidence: Lock entry present and version-identical in tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl (hashicorp/tls version=4.3.0 constraints '~> 4.0', confirmed via grep). Relocated to the opconnect state, which per the known intentional change 'replaced [the onepassword provider] with tls_private_key + a null_resource op-CLI write-back'. tls provider pin carried, moved to the state that now uses it. Same version/constraint.

elements.name: ubiquiti-community/unifi provider lock (v0.42.0-fcx1, custom patched, in-house registry)
elements.detail: Lines 133-139: tf.fusioncloudx.home/ubiquiti-community/unifi, version 0.42.0-fcx1 (custom -fcx1 patched build), constraints '0.42.0-fcx1', served from in-house registry 'tf.fusioncloudx.home', single h1: hash only (no zh: hashes — local/private registry). Drives UniFi network resources.
elements.evidence: Lock entry present and version-identical in ALL 3 tofu lock files (compute, network, opconnect: tf.fusioncloudx.home/ubiquiti-community/unifi version=0.42.0-fcx1 constraints '0.42.0-fcx1', confirmed via grep). The custom-patched provider and its private-registry source are fully preserved; tofu/PATCHED-PROVIDER.md documents the patched build (modules/unifi-host/ consumes it). Pinned in every split state. Same version/constraint/registry.
elements.tofu_location: tofu/compute/.terraform.lock.hcl + tofu/network/.terraform.lock.hcl + tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl :tf.fusioncloudx.home/ubiquiti-community/unifi (version 0.42.0-fcx1); patch documented in tofu/PATCHED-PROVIDER.md

elements.name: hashicorp/null provider lock (v3.3.0) — NEW in tofu, absent from legacy lock
elements.detail: Not present in the legacy lock file. Appears in tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl at version 3.3.0. Required by the opconnect null_resource op-CLI write-back (scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh). Noted for completeness — this is an ADDITION in tofu, the inverse of a parity gap.
elements.evidence: Legacy /tmp/legacy-tf-audit/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl has NO hashicorp/null entry (read in full, lines 1-139 — only 5 providers: onepassword, ansible, bpg/proxmox, hashicorp/tls, unifi). tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl ADDS hashicorp/null v3.3.0 (grep confirmed). This is justified by the known intentional change (opconnect Option D added a null_resource op-CLI write-back). Not a legacy element — recorded so the audit shows the tofu lock is a strict superset of legacy, with zero legacy providers dropped.

units.summary: Every functional element of the legacy terraform/ansible-inventory.tf (87 lines) is present in tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf. The 4 ansible_group resources (postgresql, application_servers, monitoring, homelab) are carried verbatim with identical names/variables/children. The two host resources — flat ansible_host.postgresql (single LXC) and ansible_host.vms (for_each over the proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm map) — are refactored into a single ansible_host.compute resource that for_each-es over a new static local.inventory_hosts map built from module outputs (module.gitlab/postgresql/mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup). The ansible provider (ansible/ansible ~> 1.3.0) is carried identically. The only behavioral change is intentional and documented: the legacy try(...,"IP not available") truthy-string failure path becomes a NULL failure path (ansible_host = null) so Jinja default() can engage, and host keys are gated by the static var.disabled_workloads rather than over computed IPs. No parity gaps.

elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:78-85 — identical name and identical variables map (ansible_user=root, python3 interpreter, same ssh_common_args).

elements.name: ansible_host.postgresql
elements.detail: Legacy single host (lines 55-67): name = proxmox_virtual_environment_container.postgresql.initialization[0].hostname; groups=[postgresql]; variables ansible_host=try(container.ipv4["eth0"],"IP not available"), vm_id=container.vm_id, type="lxc".
elements.evidence: Folded into the static local.inventory_hosts map (tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:36-44: postgresql={ip=module.postgresql.ipv4, group="postgresql", vm_id=module.postgresql.vm_id, type="lxc"}) and emitted by ansible_host.compute (lines 124-134). The container.ipv4["eth0"] lookup now lives in modules/proxmox-lxc/outputs.tf:21-22 (output "ipv4" = try(container.ipv4["eth0"], null)). Host key is now "postgresql" (each.key) rather than the resolved container hostname — both resolve to the postgresql identity; group/vm_id/type=lxc all preserved.

elements.name: ansible_host.vms (for_each = proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm)
elements.detail: Legacy (lines 73-87): for_each over the qemu-vm VM map; name=each.key; groups=[application_servers]; variables ansible_host=try(each.value.ipv4_addresses[1][0],"IP not available"), vm_id=each.value.id, type="qemu".
elements.evidence: Refactored into ansible_host.compute (tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:120 for_each = local.inventory_hosts). The single legacy qemu-vm resource (one resource, multiple VMs via for_each) is now per-app modules (gitlab/mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup), each emitting group=application_servers, type=qemu, ip=module.<x>.ipv4_address, vm_id=module.<x>.vm_id (lines 36-71). The ipv4_addresses[1][0] guest-agent lookup now lives in modules/proxmox-vm/outputs.tf:23-24 (output ipv4_address = try(one(...ipv4_addresses)[1][0], null)). All QEMU hosts land in application_servers with type=qemu — equivalent.

elements.name: for_each over computed VM map (legacy line 74)
elements.detail: Legacy used for_each = proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm (the resource map). Keys derived from the VM resource. The tofu version deliberately moves the for_each onto a STATIC, plan-time-known map keyed only by static var.disabled_workloads so keys never depend on computed IPs.
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:120 (for_each = local.inventory_hosts) over local.inventory_hosts (lines 33-72), where keys are gated by contains(var.disabled_workloads,...) — a plan-time-known input. Documented in header comment lines 7-17. Functionally equivalent inventory output; fixes the 'for_each over computed values' plan failure.

elements.name: try(..., "IP not available") fallback for ansible_host
elements.detail: Legacy used try(<computed ip>, "IP not available") for both the LXC (line 60-63) and QEMU (line 80-83) hosts — a truthy STRING fallback when no IP lease exists.
elements.evidence: Intentionally changed to a NULL failure path. Module outputs return null on missing lease (modules/proxmox-vm/outputs.tf:24 try(...,null); modules/proxmox-lxc/outputs.tf:22 try(...,null)) and ansible_host = each.value.ip flows null through (tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:131). Documented as 'Footgun #3' in header comment lines 18-22: null lets Jinja default() engage instead of a truthy 'IP not available' string. Behavioral improvement, not a gap.

elements.name: ansible_host (per-host IP variable)
elements.detail: Both legacy hosts set variables.ansible_host to the guest IP. LXC: container.ipv4[eth0]; QEMU: ipv4_addresses[1][0].
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:131 (ansible_host = each.value.ip), value sourced from module ipv4/ipv4_address outputs per host in local.inventory_hosts.

elements.name: vm_id (per-host VMID variable)
elements.detail: Legacy LXC sets vm_id=container.vm_id (line 64); legacy QEMU sets vm_id=each.value.id (line 84).
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:132 (vm_id = each.value.vm_id), sourced from module.<x>.vm_id outputs (modules/proxmox-vm/outputs.tf:11-12, modules/proxmox-lxc/outputs.tf:6-7). Note legacy QEMU used .id whereas tofu uses module vm_id output — both yield the Proxmox VMID. VMID Scheme B values applied per known intentional change.

elements.name: type (lxc/qemu discriminator variable)
elements.detail: Legacy LXC host sets type="lxc" (line 65); legacy QEMU hosts set type="qemu" (line 85).
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:133 (type = each.value.type); postgresql entry type="lxc" (line 43), all app entries type="qemu" (lines 39,49,55,61,67).

elements.name: ansible provider (cloud.terraform plugin) version ~> 1.3.0
elements.detail: The whole file depends on the ansible/ansible provider (the cloud.terraform.terraform_provider inventory plugin). Legacy declared source=ansible/ansible version ~> 1.3.0 in terraform/provider.tf:13-16; provider block usage implicit.
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/providers.tf:27-31 declares ansible = { source="ansible/ansible", version="~> 1.3.0" } — identical pin — and providers.tf:63 provider "ansible" {}. Confirmed legacy pin matches: grep terraform/provider.tf:13-16 shows same source + ~> 1.3.0.

elements.kind: implicit dependency on qemu-vm host source
elements.name: proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm (the for_each source map)
elements.detail: Legacy file's QEMU host loop depended on the single proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm resource (a map of all VMs). That resource itself lives in qemu-vm.tf, but the inventory's wiring to it (each.value.ipv4_addresses/.id) is the in-file element.
elements.evidence: The flat qemu-vm map is replaced by per-app proxmox-vm module instances (module.gitlab/mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup) referenced in local.inventory_hosts (tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:36-71). Inventory now consumes module outputs (ipv4_address, vm_id) rather than indexing the shared VM map. Functionally equivalent host enumeration.

elements.name: monitoring group hosts
elements.detail: In the legacy file the monitoring group is defined but NO ansible_host is assigned to it (no host references ansible_group.monitoring.name). It exists only as a child of homelab.
elements.evidence: tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf — same: group defined (96-103), referenced as homelab child (110), but no host in local.inventory_hosts uses group="monitoring" (all are postgresql or application_servers). Identical empty-group behavior preserved.

units.summary: Legacy terraform/backend.tf is a 6-line file defining a single terraform{} block with a backend "local" configuration that stored state at relative path terraform.tfstate. Per the KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE (replaced local backend with S3+SSE-KMS remote backend, per-state backend.tf, split into 3 states), this functionality is refactored into three per-state S3 backends: tofu/network/backend.tf, tofu/opconnect/backend.tf, tofu/compute/backend.tf. No parity gaps. The state-storage responsibility is fully carried; the local-file mechanism was intentionally replaced with S3+KMS, and the single state was deliberately split into 3.

elements.name: terraform { backend "local" } block
elements.detail: Legacy line 1-6: a terraform{} block containing backend "local" {}. State-storage configuration responsibility. Refactored from a single local backend into three per-state S3 backends (S3+SSE-KMS), each a terraform{ backend "s3" {} } block. This is a KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE (local backend replaced with S3+SSE-KMS remote backend, per-state backend.tf, split into 3 states).
elements.evidence: Legacy /tmp/legacy-tf-audit/terraform/backend.tf:1-6 (backend "local"). Tofu equivalents: tofu/network/backend.tf:2-13 (backend "s3", key onprem/proxmox/network/terraform.tfstate), tofu/opconnect/backend.tf:2-13 (key onprem/proxmox/opconnect/terraform.tfstate), tofu/compute/backend.tf:2-13 (key onprem/proxmox/compute/terraform.tfstate). All three: bucket tmpx-tfstate-065094257518-use2, region us-east-2, encrypt=true, kms_key_id arn:aws:kms:us-east-2:065094257518:key/1d876310..., use_lockfile=true, assume_role role_arn OrganizationAccountAccessRole.

elements.name: path = "terraform.tfstate" (local backend state path)
elements.detail: Legacy line 4: path argument of backend "local" — the relative on-disk location of the state file (terraform/terraform.tfstate). In the S3 backend this concept is replaced by the bucket + key pair that addresses the state object in S3. Each of the 3 states gets a distinct S3 key namespaced under onprem/proxmox/<state>/terraform.tfstate. Refactored as part of the local->S3 backend migration KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE.
elements.evidence: Legacy /tmp/legacy-tf-audit/terraform/backend.tf:4 (path = "terraform.tfstate"). Tofu replacements (bucket+key): tofu/network/backend.tf:3-4 (bucket=tmpx-tfstate-065094257518-use2, key=onprem/proxmox/network/terraform.tfstate); tofu/opconnect/backend.tf:3-4 (key=onprem/proxmox/opconnect/terraform.tfstate); tofu/compute/backend.tf:3-4 (key=onprem/proxmox/compute/terraform.tfstate).

units.summary: The legacy file is a single proxmox_virtual_environment_file resource (gitlab_vendor_data_cloud_config) holding one inline #cloud-config vendor-data document for the gitlab VM. The entire file was refactored into the parameterized modules/cloud-init module, invoked as module "gitlab_cloud_init" in tofu/compute/gitlab.tf. The resource, datastore, file naming, hostname/fqdn/timezone, package_update/upgrade, the package set, and debconf_selections all carry cleanly. The vendor-data is now rendered via yamlencode rather than a literal heredoc, but functionally equivalent. The one real gap: the legacy GitLab-specific runcmd block (enable/start ssh, dpkg-reconfigure postfix, the cloud-init-verify.log smoke tests, /tmp/cloud-config.done marker, and /var/lib/cloud-init.gitlab.ready marker) is NOT carried — the gitlab_cloud_init module call does not pass a custom runcmd, so it falls back to the module default which only enables/starts qemu-guest-agent. These runcmd items have no known intentional-change justification, so they are flagged MISSING (the postfix dpkg-reconfigure is the highest-impact loss since postfix is still installed but never reconfigured non-interactively at boot).

units.summary: Both legacy cloud-init resources (user_data + vendor_data snippet files) were refactored into the reusable modules/cloud-init module, consumed per-app across the split states (gitlab/immich/mealie/runitup/tandoor in compute, opconnect in opconnect). Nearly every directive is carried verbatim or refactored 1:1. Two notable deltas: (1) the legacy single shared vendor-data file became one per-VM vendor-data file (refactored, equivalent behavior + improved per-host fqdn/upgrade/debconf support); (2) the vendor-data runcmd line `echo "done" > /tmp/cloud-config.done` was dropped with no justification (MISSING, minor — the real provisioning marker /var/lib/cloud-init.provision.ready in user-data IS carried). Also corrected: the for_each gating on local.enabled_vm_configs (driven by var.disabled_workloads) was NOT dropped despite the KNOWN-CHANGE note; disabled_workloads survives in compute as per-app count guards with a validation block.

elements.name: proxmox_virtual_environment_file.user_data_cloud_config

Refactored into modules/cloud-init resource proxmox_virtual_environment_file.user_data, instantiated per-app via explicit module calls (module.gitlab_cloud_init, module.immich_cloud_init, module.mealie_cloud_init, module.runitup_cloud_init, module.tandoor_cloud_init in compute; module.opconnect_cloud_init in opconnect). The for_each fan-out is replaced by named per-app module blocks. Behavior equivalent.

Built via yamlencode(local.vendor_data_config) instead of a heredoc, but same #cloud-config content. Per-VM instancing is a structural change enabling per-host fqdn/package_upgrade/debconf; for the base apps it yields equivalent vendor-data. Behavior equivalent (each VM gets the same base directives).

New design replaces the centralized for_each with explicit per-app cloud-init module calls, each guarded by count = contains(var.disabled_workloads, "<svc>") ? 0 : 1 (gitlab is a protected singleton with NO count, always materialized). disabled_workloads survives in compute as a real variable WITH a validation block restricting it to the disposable set (mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup). NOTE: the KNOWN-INTENTIONAL-CHANGES note lists 'disabled_workloads handling' as removed, but the gating mechanism was in fact carried/refactored, not dropped.

units.summary: All functional elements of the legacy flat terraform/dns.tf were carried into the new tofu tree via a refactor: the two unifi_client resources + two unifi_dns_record resources collapsed into a single reusable modules/unifi-host module (one unifi_client.this + one unifi_dns_record.this), invoked once per host. The legacy for_each-over-fcx_vms fan-out is replaced by explicit per-service module calls in tofu/compute (gitlab_dns, mealie_dns, tandoor_dns, immich_dns, runitup_dns, postgresql_dns) plus tofu/opconnect (opconnect_dns). Every behavior is preserved: lower(mac) normalization, allow_existing=true observe-then-pin adoption, network_id omitted (null) for the round-trip quirk, record_type="A", enabled=true, the <name>.fusioncloudx.home A record, and live-IP sourcing (VM guest-agent ipv4_addresses[1][0] / mac_addresses[1] now in proxmox-vm/outputs.tf; LXC ipv4["eth0"] / network_interface[0].mac_address now in proxmox-lxc/outputs.tf). The locals that resolved IP-once (vm_ip/lxc_ip) and the fcx_vms set are subsumed by the module's var.ip/var.mac inputs and the per-service count gating on var.disabled_workloads. No parity gaps. The only count difference is the host roster (legacy named 6 VMs incl. backrest/duplicati conceptually; new tree drops the backup VMs per the P1 backup-stack kill and adds opconnect as a new TF-managed host) — all intentional/expected, not a dns.tf functional loss.

New tree has no central fcx_vms set; the fan-out is replaced by explicit per-service module calls (modules/unifi-host) in tofu/compute/{gitlab,mealie,tandoor,immich,runitup}.tf and postgresql.tf. The disabled_workloads gating that fcx_vms encoded survives per-service: tofu/compute/mealie.tf:38 'count = contains(var.disabled_workloads, "mealie") ? 0 : 1' on the *_dns module (same for tandoor.tf:36, immich.tf:39, runitup.tf:39). So a disabled workload's DNS+reservation vanish exactly as fcx_vms did.

units.summary: The legacy file defines exactly one functional element: a single proxmox_virtual_environment_download_file resource ("debian12_lxc_template") that downloads the Debian 12 standard LXC vztmpl to the nas-infrastructure datastore on node pve. No variables, outputs, locals, providers, modules, data sources, lifecycle blocks, meta-args, provisioners, dynamic blocks, or cloud-init directives exist in the file (it is 17 lines, all comments + the one resource). The resource is fully carried into the new 3-state layout at tofu/network/templates.tf:95-109 with identical node_name/content_type/datastore_id/url. Two attribute VALUES are intentionally flipped (overwrite true->false, overwrite_unmanaged false->true) to adopt the already-downloaded file under the new S3 remote state instead of clobbering it — a deliberate migration-adoption change, not a parity gap. The migration also ADDED an output (debian_lxc_template_file_id) wiring the file id across states to compute/postgresql.tf's proxmox-lxc module. NOTE: the on-disk legacy state stub (/tmp/legacy-tf-audit/ondisk/terraform.tfstate) is a 17-line empty state with no resources, so it could not be used to cross-check applied values; the legacy .tf source is authoritative here.

elements.name: proxmox_virtual_environment_download_file.debian12_lxc_template
elements.detail: Downloads Debian 12 standard LXC template (vztmpl). Legacy attrs: node_name="pve", content_type="vztmpl", datastore_id="nas-infrastructure", url="http://download.proxmox.com/images/system/debian-12-standard_12.12-1_amd64.tar.zst", overwrite=true, overwrite_unmanaged=false. Carried into the network state (P1 foundation templates) with the SAME resource name and identical node_name/content_type/datastore_id/url. Two attribute values intentionally flipped: overwrite true->false and overwrite_unmanaged false->true (tofu/network/templates.tf:107-108) to ADOPT the pre-existing on-NAS file under the new S3+SSE-KMS remote backend instead of erroring/re-downloading. Behavior-equivalent for the goal (vztmpl present on nas-infrastructure). The proxmox provider that authors it is present in network/providers.tf:24 (bpg/proxmox 0.107.0).
elements.evidence: Resource present at tofu/network/templates.tf:95-109 (matched node_name pve / content_type vztmpl / datastore_id nas-infrastructure / identical Debian 12.12-1 url). Provider block tofu/network/providers.tf:5-7,24 (bpg/proxmox 0.107.0). grep across tree confirms single definition. overwrite/overwrite_unmanaged flip documented inline at templates.tf:102-108.

elements.name: debian_lxc_template_file_id

Exposes proxmox_virtual_environment_download_file.debian12_lxc_template.id from the network state so the compute state's proxmox-lxc module can consume it as template_file_id (compute/postgresql.tf:25 -> data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.debian_lxc_template_file_id). This is added cross-state wiring introduced by the 3-state split, not a legacy element being audited; recorded for completeness as added-functionality serving the same template.
elements.evidence: tofu/network/outputs.tf:17-20; consumed at tofu/compute/postgresql.tf:25 via tofu/compute/remote-state.tf:15. Not present in legacy file (legacy file has no outputs). This is the cross-state replacement for what was an implicit same-state resource reference in the flat legacy layout.

units.summary: The single legacy resource `proxmox_virtual_environment_container.postgresql` was refactored into the `modules/proxmox-lxc` module (resource `this`) consumed by `tofu/compute/postgresql.tf` as `module.postgresql`. Nearly every functional element is carried or cleanly refactored: node_name, vm_id (2001→2101 Scheme B), started/start_on_boot, unprivileged=true, operating_system (debian template via network remote-state), cpu, memory+swap, disk (datastore vm-data→local-zfs greenfield lock), network_interface eth0/vmbr0 DHCP, console, initialization (hostname + dhcp + injected SSH pubkey, now read from 1Password via Option D instead of generated tls_private_key), features.nesting, and tags. The module also ADDS an unconditional lifecycle.prevent_destroy=true seatbelt (new hardening, not a legacy feature) and a co-located postgresql_dns UniFi A-record/DHCP reservation (new). ONE real gap: the container `description` field (legacy line 19, value "Centralized PostgreSQL database server for homelab services") is set on NO resource in the new tree — absent from both the module and the call-site, and not justified by any KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE.

units.summary: All 8 1Password items from legacy terraform/onepassword.tf are fully accounted for with no parity gaps. The 7 password_recipe credential items (postgresql_admin, mealie_db_user, tandoor_db_user, gitlab_root_password, gitlab_runner_token, tandoor_secret_key, immich_db_password) are carried verbatim into tofu/compute/secrets.tf (category/type/hostname/port/database/username/password_recipe/note_value/tags all preserved; only cosmetic changes: tag prefix terraform->opentofu, DB FQDN hardcoded to a value-equivalent local, provenance string rebranded OpenTofu). The ansible_ssh_key secure_note item is refactored under the known opconnect Option D change: instead of the onepassword provider resource, the keypair is generated in opconnect (tls_private_key.ansible) and the IDENTICAL item (same title, 2 sections, 4 fields with matching labels+types) is written to 1Password via the desktop op CLI (scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh) and read back by compute via a data source — the private key now stays out of compute state. The op_managed_by local is carried. The legacy timestamp()+ignore_changes idempotency pattern is intentionally dropped (footgun #8) in favor of a static note + fingerprint-keyed no-op trigger. No secret resource silently vanished.

elements.name: op_managed_by
elements.detail: Heredoc provenance stamp applied as note_value to every recipe-based 1Password item. Legacy text: 'Managed by Terraform — thisisbramiller/FusionCloudX-Infrastructure (terraform/onepassword.tf). Do not edit by hand; manual changes are overwritten on `terraform apply`.'
elements.evidence: Legacy terraform/onepassword.tf:8-15. Carried at tofu/compute/secrets.tf:21-27 with text rebranded to 'Managed by OpenTofu — ...(tofu/compute/secrets.tf). ...overwritten on `tofu apply`.' Behaviorally identical (static provenance note, no timestamp). Referenced as note_value at secrets.tf:41,60,79,102,116,133,150 (7 items).

elements.name: ansible_ssh_key
elements.detail: Legacy secure_note item titled 'Infrastructure Ansible SSH Key', tags [terraform,ansible,ssh,infrastructure], note_value with embedded timestamp(), 2 sections (Private Key / Public Key) and 4 fields (private_key CONCEALED, key_type STRING, public_key STRING, public_key_fingerprint_sha256 STRING), values sourced from tls_private_key.ansible. Lifecycle ignore_changes=[note_value]. opconnect Option D removed the onepassword PROVIDER from the secrets ROOT, so this item is no longer a TF provider resource: the keypair is generated in opconnect (tls_private_key.ansible) and the SAME item (same title, same 2 sections, same 4 fields/types, equivalent tags ['opentofu','ansible','ssh','infrastructure'], a static NOTES field) is written to 1Password via the desktop op CLI in account mode (Connect-independent). compute READS it as a data source (never re-creating it, so the private key never lands in compute state).
elements.evidence: Legacy terraform/onepassword.tf:21-74. Refactored: keypair gen + write-back at tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:28-61 (tls_private_key.ansible:28-30; null_resource.ansible_ssh_key_writeback:38-61 passes SSH_PRIVATE_KEY/SSH_PUBLIC_KEY/KEY_TYPE=ED25519/SSH_PUBLIC_KEY_FP via env). The op-CLI template at tofu/opconnect/scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh:65-83 reproduces every legacy field/section: sections sec_private 'Private Key' + sec_public 'Public Key'; fields private_key/CONCEALED, key_type/STRING, public_key/STRING, public_key_fingerprint_sha256/STRING — exact label+type+section match. compute reads it via data source at tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:21-39 (pulls public_key STRING field by label). Item title carried via var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title (opconnect/variables.tf:16, compute/variables.tf:38).

elements.name: ansible_ssh_key.lifecycle.ignore_changes=[note_value]
elements.detail: Legacy used ignore_changes=[note_value] to suppress perpetual drift from the timestamp() embedded in note_value. Since the new design uses a STATIC note (no timestamp), there is nothing to ignore — the write-back is idempotent via a fingerprint-keyed trigger + a script-level no-op when the stored fingerprint already matches.
elements.evidence: Legacy terraform/onepassword.tf:71-73. No lifecycle block exists in tofu (grep tofu/compute/secrets.tf, tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf, tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf -> '(no lifecycle blocks)'). Equivalent no-op behavior achieved instead by null_resource trigger fingerprint=tls_private_key.ansible.public_key_fingerprint_sha256 (opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:40) and the script idempotency check (op-write-ssh-key.sh:46-51). Justified by footgun #8 (NO timestamp() — keep apply a no-op), called out in code comments at opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:35 and secrets.tf:10.
elements.drop_reason: Footgun #8 — note_value made static (no timestamp()), so ignore_changes is moot; idempotency moved to a fingerprint-keyed null_resource trigger + script-level no-op. Equivalent no-op-on-reapply behavior.

ONE root output is MISSING with no justification: ansible_ssh_key_fingerprint (the SHA256 fingerprint output) — its value is still computed/used internally in opconnect's ssh-key write-back but never re-exposed as an output. Additionally TWO sub-element gaps are MISSING: infrastructure_summary.databases and postgresql_connection.databases, both because var.postgresql_databases was removed entirely from the tofu tree (zero references) and that removal is not covered by any KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE. Justified drops: onepassword_items.ssh.ansible_key (Option D: now a data-source READ, not a managed onepassword_item resource) and the cosmetic per-VM/container cpu/memory/disk/status summary fields.

units.summary: terraform/PATCHED-PROVIDER.md is a documentation/runbook file (no HCL resources, variables, outputs, or providers of its own) describing the patched UniFi provider supply-chain mechanism: a maintained fork (thisisbramiller/terraform-provider-unifi @ patches), built into a filesystem mirror under a synthetic source host (tf.fusioncloudx.home/ubiquiti-community/unifi @ 0.42.0-fcx1), pinned via h1: checksums in .terraform.lock.hcl, replacing dev_overrides. The entire doc was carried to tofu/PATCHED-PROVIDER.md as a near-verbatim copy. The ONLY diffs are (a) Terraform->OpenTofu and `terraform`->`tofu` CLI renames, (b) the single-state `cd terraform` build step replaced with multi-state `cd tofu/network ... repeat for compute` (reflecting the 3-state split), and (c) an ADDED `.tofurc` committed at repo root for CI portability (the legacy doc only mentioned `~/.terraformrc`). Every mechanism the doc describes is concretely present in the target tree: provider source declarations in tofu/{network,opconnect,compute}/providers.tf + modules/unifi-host/providers.tf, version pin 0.42.0-fcx1, real h1: checksums in all 3 lockfiles, the build script at scripts/build-unifi-provider.sh, and the new .tofurc. No parity gaps.

units.summary: Legacy terraform/provider.tf is a pure root-level provider-plumbing file: one terraform{} block (required_version + 5 required_providers) plus three configured provider blocks (proxmox, onepassword, unifi). It declares NO resources/data/modules/variables/outputs/locals. Every functional element was carried into the tofu/ tree, refactored only by the intentional 3-state split (network/opconnect/compute each gets its own providers.tf) and the opconnect Option-D drop of the onepassword PROVIDER. The proxmox and unifi provider config blocks are reproduced verbatim across all three states; the patched-unifi mirror plumbing survives intact (same synthetic source host + 0.42.0-fcx1 pin, PATCHED-PROVIDER.md present). The only genuine value change is required_version >=1.9 -> >=1.8, a side effect of removing the backup-stack cross-variable validation that drove the 1.9 floor. No parity gaps (MISSING) found.

elements.name: terraform { required_version = ">= 1.9" }
elements.detail: Legacy floor was >= 1.9 specifically because cross-variable validation (disabled_workloads -> var.vm_configs) required 1.9+. That validation belonged to the killed backup-stack / disabled_workloads handling, so the floor was relaxed to >= 1.8. Carried into every state and module providers.tf, just at the lower bound.
elements.evidence: legacy provider.tf:1-2 (>= 1.9, comment cites disabled_workloads->vm_configs). tofu now: tofu/compute/providers.tf:9, tofu/network/providers.tf:2, tofu/opconnect/providers.tf:17 all '>= 1.8'; also modules/{cloud-init,proxmox-lxc,proxmox-vm,unifi-host}/providers.tf:4/8. The 1.9-only validation feature is part of disabled_workloads handling (KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE: backup stack removed).

units.summary: terraform/qemu-vm.tf (the flat for_each VM resource that built all app VMs) was fully carried into the new tofu tree, refactored into the proxmox-vm module (one module call per app, with a protected/disposable count-split that adds prevent_destroy hardening on gitlab/opconnect). Every functional element — scalar args, agent/memory/cpu/operating_system/serial_device blocks, DHCP cloud-init init, ignore_changes lifecycle, gitlab vendor_data branching, and the ansible-inventory/dns/outputs wiring described in the header — is present and equivalent. All deltas (VMID Scheme B, clone 1000->9001 via remote state, depends_on -> 3-state ordering, datastore vm-data->local-zfs, init qcow2 dropped, terraform->opentofu tags) match documented intentional changes. The ONLY item needing human confirmation: the legacy auto-attached 32GB scsi1 extra disk for non-default-datastore VMs (immich) is now an opt-in extra_disk_size_gb knob that NO app sets, so immich receives no extra disk — likely intentional (photos on NFS, DB on local-zfs) but unconfirmed.

elements.name: local.enabled_vm_configs
elements.detail: Legacy comprehension filtering var.vm_configs by !contains(var.disabled_workloads,k) — the single source of truth for the VM set, driving for_each on qemu-vm + cloud-init + dns.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:5-10. In tofu, the map+for_each model was decomposed into one-module-call-per-app. The disabled_workloads filter is now expressed per-app as count = contains(var.disabled_workloads,"mealie")?0:1 on each disposable module (tofu/compute/mealie.tf:12, tandoor.tf:12, immich.tf:12, runitup.tf:12). Protected singletons (gitlab/opconnect) are always built (no count). Equivalent net selection behavior.

elements.name: proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.qemu-vm (for_each VM resource)
elements.detail: The single flat for_each resource that built every app VM is now the proxmox-vm module, instantiated once per app. Module splits into byte-identical protected/disposable count-gated resources.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:12-83. Now modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:28 (protected) + :99 (disposable), called by tofu/compute/gitlab.tf:13, mealie.tf:10, tandoor.tf:10, immich.tf:11, runitup.tf:11, and tofu/opconnect/opconnect.tf:19.

elements.name: for_each = local.enabled_vm_configs
elements.detail: for_each over the enabled VM map. Replaced by explicit one-module-call-per-VM (gitlab/mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup/opconnect) with the disposable-vs-protected toggle expressed via count on each call.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:13. No for_each in module; instead per-app module calls each with their own count guard. grep proxmox-vm module callers confirms 6 explicit instantiations.

elements.name: vm_id = each.value.vm_id
elements.detail: Per-VM Proxmox VMID. Legacy values gitlab 1103, mealie 1104, tandoor 1105, immich 1106, runitup 1111. Now hardcoded per-app under VMID Scheme B.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:15 + variables.tf vm_configs. Now var.vm_id (modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:1) wired per app: gitlab 1201 (gitlab.tf:16), mealie 1301 (mealie.tf:14), tandoor 1302 (tandoor.tf:14), immich 1303 (immich.tf:15), runitup 1304 (runitup.tf:15), opconnect 1101 (opconnect.tf:21).
elements.drop_reason: VMID Scheme B applied per KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE (gitlab 1201, mealie 1301, tandoor 1302, immich 1303, runitup 1304, opconnect 1101).

elements.name: name = each.value.name
elements.detail: VM/guest name passed through.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:16. Now var.name → name = var.name (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:32,103), set per app (e.g. mealie.tf:15 name="mealie").

elements.name: node_name = "pve"
elements.detail: Target Proxmox node hardcoded to pve.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:17. Now local.vm_node_name="pve" (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:19) → node_name=local.vm_node_name (:33,104).

elements.name: started = each.value.started
elements.detail: Whether VM is started after creation. Legacy all true.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:18. Now var.started (default true, modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:49-53) → started=var.started (:34,105). App calls omit it → default true, matching legacy started=true for all.

elements.name: on_boot = try(each.value.on_boot, true)
elements.detail: Start-on-host-boot. Legacy optional with default true.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:19 + variables.tf:20 optional(bool,true). Now var.on_boot (default true, modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:55-59) → on_boot=var.on_boot (:35,106). opconnect explicitly sets on_boot=true (opconnect.tf:32); others default.

elements.name: tags = ["terraform", "ubuntu"]
elements.detail: Guest tags. Legacy static ["terraform","ubuntu"]. Now parameterized var.tags (default ["opentofu","ubuntu"]); apps pass ["opentofu","ubuntu",<app>]. "terraform"→"opentofu" is the tooling rename; per-app tag added.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:20. Now var.tags (modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:43-47, default ["opentofu","ubuntu"]) → tags=var.tags (:36,107). e.g. mealie.tf:18 tags=["opentofu","ubuntu","mealie"].

elements.name: bios = "seabios"
elements.detail: BIOS type seabios.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:21. Now local.vm_bios="seabios" (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:20) → bios=local.vm_bios (:37,108).

elements.name: depends_on = [proxmox_virtual_environment_vm.ubuntu-template]
elements.detail: Ordering against the ubuntu-template build. Template moved to network/ state (applied first, P3); ordering now enforced by the state split + the template_vm_id remote-state reference rather than an in-state depends_on.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:23. No depends_on in module; instead template_vm_id = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.ubuntu_template_vm_id (gitlab.tf:25, mealie.tf:23, etc.) → tofu/network/outputs.tf:12-14 ubuntu_template_vm_id from network/templates.tf. Cross-state ordering replaces the direct resource dependency.
elements.drop_reason: Replaced by the network/opconnect/compute 3-state split (KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE): template authored in network/ (first state), consumed via remote-state output; intra-state depends_on no longer applicable.

elements.name: clone { vm_id = 1000; full = each.value.full_clone; retries = 10 }
elements.detail: Full-clone from the ubuntu template. Legacy source VMID 1000, full=optional(true), retries=10.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:25-29. Now clone{ vm_id=var.template_vm_id; full=true; retries=local.vm_clone_retries } (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:39-43,110-114). template_vm_id sourced from network remote state (resolves to Scheme B 9001, replacing 1000). full hardcoded true (legacy default was true; legacy never set it false). retries=10 via local.vm_clone_retries (main.tf:22).
elements.drop_reason: Clone source 1000→9001 per VMID Scheme B (template now ubuntu-template=9001 in network state).

elements.name: agent { enabled = true }
elements.detail: QEMU guest agent enabled.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:31-33. Now agent{enabled=true} (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:45-47,116-118).

elements.name: memory { dedicated = each.value.memory_mb }
elements.detail: Dedicated memory. Legacy per-VM (gitlab 16384, mealie/tandoor 2048, immich 8192, runitup 4096).
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:35-37. Now memory{dedicated=var.memory_mb} (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:49-51,120-122). Per-app values match legacy: gitlab 16384 (gitlab.tf:19), mealie 2048 (mealie.tf:17), tandoor 2048 (tandoor.tf:17), immich 8192 (immich.tf:18), runitup 4096 (runitup.tf:18).

elements.name: cpu { cores = each.value.cpu_cores; type = "x86-64-v2-AES" }
elements.detail: CPU cores + type. Legacy per-VM cores (gitlab 8, mealie/tandoor 2, immich/runitup 4), type x86-64-v2-AES.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:39-42. Now cpu{cores=var.cores; type=local.vm_cpu_type} (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:53-56,124-127), local.vm_cpu_type="x86-64-v2-AES" (:21). Per-app cores match legacy: gitlab 8 (gitlab.tf:18), mealie 2 (mealie.tf:16), tandoor 2, immich 4, runitup 4.

elements.name: dynamic disk (extra OS disk scsi1 when datastore_id != "vm-data")
elements.detail: Conditionally attaches a 32GB raw scsi1 OS disk on a non-default datastore. Legacy gate: datastore_id != "vm-data". Legacy hardcoded size=32.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:46-54. Now dynamic disk over local.vm_extra_disk_set = var.extra_disk_size_gb!=null?[1]:[] (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:24,60-68,131-139); size=var.extra_disk_size_gb, interface scsi1, file_format raw. Gate changed from datastore-comparison to an explicit extra_disk_size_gb!=null knob (default null). NOTE: no current app sets extra_disk_size_gb, so this dynamic disk is INERT in every present call (legacy immich, the only datastore_id=local-zfs VM, WOULD have triggered the legacy disk; in tofu immich.tf does not pass extra_disk_size_gb). Behavioral divergence flagged below.

elements.name: extra scsi1 disk no longer auto-triggered for non-default-datastore VMs (immich)
elements.detail: Legacy auto-attached a 32GB scsi1 disk to ANY VM whose datastore_id != "vm-data" — in practice immich (datastore_id=local-zfs). In tofu the disk is gated on the new extra_disk_size_gb knob (default null) and NO app sets it, so immich (now datastore_id=local-zfs, the disposable variant) gets NO extra disk. The legacy auto-disk for immich appears intentionally re-expressed as an opt-in knob, but no consumer opts in — this is a potential parity gap for immich's storage layout unless the immich photo/DB disk is now provisioned elsewhere (comment says photo library mounts UNAS NFS via Ansible; DB+Docker I/O on local-zfs). Cannot confirm the extra 32GB OS disk is intentionally dropped vs accidentally lost — flagging for human confirmation.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:46-54 (gate datastore_id!="vm-data"; immich had datastore_id=local-zfs per variables.tf:54 → legacy attached the disk). tofu: immich.tf:11-26 sets datastore_id=local-zfs but does NOT pass extra_disk_size_gb → module local.vm_extra_disk_set=[] → no disk. Searched all proxmox-vm callers for extra_disk_size_gb: absent in every call.

elements.name: initialization { datastore_id = "vm-data"; file_format = "qcow2"; ip_config ipv4=dhcp; user_data_file_id; vendor_data_file_id }
elements.detail: Cloud-init init disk on vm-data (qcow2), DHCP ipv4, and the user/vendor data snippet IDs. In tofu datastore_id=var.datastore_id (defaults local-zfs per greenfield storage lock) and the explicit file_format="qcow2" on initialization was dropped (provider default).
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:56-69. Now initialization{ datastore_id=var.datastore_id; ip_config{ipv4{address="dhcp"}}; user_data_file_id=var.user_data_file_id; vendor_data_file_id=var.vendor_data_file_id } (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:70-81,141-152). file_format="qcow2" NOT present (grep modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf file_format → only the raw extra-disk). datastore_id default local-zfs (variables.tf:21-24 with comment 'the flat config used vm-data').
elements.drop_reason: datastore_id vm-data→local-zfs is the documented greenfield storage lock (variables.tf:24); init file_format=qcow2 dropped to provider default as part of the same storage-lock migration.

elements.name: initialization.ip_config.ipv4.address = "dhcp"
elements.detail: DHCP IPv4 addressing for cloud-init.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:60-64. Now ip_config{ipv4{address="dhcp"}} (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:73-77,144-148).

elements.name: initialization.user_data_file_id = proxmox_virtual_environment_file.user_data_cloud_config[each.key].id
elements.detail: Per-VM cloud-init user-data snippet wiring. Legacy keyed off the for_each per-VM user_data file resource.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:66. Now user_data_file_id=var.user_data_file_id (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:79,150), each app passing module.<app>_cloud_init.user_data_file_id (gitlab.tf:27, mealie.tf:24, etc.). user-data resource itself moved into the cloud-init module (out of scope for this unit; lives in legacy cloud-init.tf).

elements.name: initialization.vendor_data_file_id (gitlab-specific ternary)
elements.detail: Legacy ternary: gitlab gets gitlab_vendor_data_cloud_config, all others get the standard vendor_data_cloud_config. Now each app gets its OWN cloud-init module instance producing its own vendor_data_file_id, so gitlab's prereqs (postfix/debconf/extra_packages) flow through gitlab_cloud_init instead of an inline ternary.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:67-68 (each.key=="gitlab"?gitlab_vendor_data:standard). Now vendor_data_file_id=var.vendor_data_file_id (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:80,151); gitlab.tf:28 passes module.gitlab_cloud_init.vendor_data_file_id, and gitlab_cloud_init (gitlab.tf:31-56) carries the gitlab-specific debconf_selections + extra_packages. Per-app cloud-init module produces vendor_data_file_id (modules/cloud-init/outputs.tf:6). The gitlab-vs-standard branching is now structural (separate module instances), not a ternary.

elements.name: operating_system { type = "l26" }
elements.detail: Linux 2.6+ guest OS type.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:71-73. Now local.vm_os_type="l26" (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:23) → operating_system{type=local.vm_os_type} (:83-85,154-156).

elements.name: serial_device {}
elements.detail: Empty serial device (required for cloud-init console).
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:75. Now serial_device{} (modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:87,158).

elements.name: lifecycle { ignore_changes = [initialization] }
elements.detail: Ignore cloud-init state drift on initialization.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:77-81. Now present in BOTH module variants: modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:89-94 (protected) and :160-164 (disposable), each with ignore_changes=[initialization].

elements.name: lifecycle.prevent_destroy (NEW, not in legacy)
elements.detail: Not a legacy element — noted for completeness. The legacy qemu-vm resource had NO prevent_destroy. The module ADDS prevent_destroy=true on the 'protected' variant (gitlab/opconnect singletons) via a literal. This is additive hardening, not a legacy feature being carried; no legacy parity concern.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf lifecycle block (:77-81) only had ignore_changes. tofu modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf:90 prevent_destroy=true (protected only). Additive.

elements.name: ansible-inventory follows the qemu-vm resource
elements.detail: Header comment (lines 1-4) states ansible-inventory follows the qemu-vm resource. The inventory now consumes per-app module outputs (ip/vm_id) with disposables gated by disabled_workloads, matching legacy behavior.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:1-4 (comment). tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf:33-68 builds host entries from module.gitlab/mealie/tandoor/immich/runitup outputs (ipv4_address, vm_id, type=qemu), disposables gated by contains(var.disabled_workloads,...) (lines 46,52,58,64). one(module.<svc>[*].x) handles count=0.

elements.name: dns.tf fcx_vms keys off the VM set
elements.detail: Header comment (lines 1-2) notes dns.tf fcx_vms keys off the enabled VM set. Now each app declares its own unifi-host DNS module (per-host DHCP reservation + A record), MAC/IP passed explicitly from the VM module — replacing the centralized fcx_vms map.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:1-2 (comment). tofu: per-app module.<app>_dns (modules/unifi-host) — gitlab.tf:61-68, mealie.tf:36-43, tandoor.tf, immich.tf, runitup.tf — mac=module.<app>.mac_address, ip=module.<app>.ipv4_address.

elements.name: outputs try()-guarded for excluded VMs
elements.detail: Header comment (lines 2-3) notes outputs are try()-guarded for excluded VMs. Now expressed via one(module.<svc>[*].x) (null when count=0) for disposables and try()-guarded attribute access in the module's own ipv4/mac outputs.
elements.evidence: Legacy qemu-vm.tf:2-3 (comment). tofu/compute/ansible-inventory.tf uses one(module.<svc>[*]...) for disposables (lines 47,49,53,55,59,61,65,67). Module outputs.tf:23,29 wrap ipv4/mac in try(...,null). Equivalent excluded-VM safety.

units.summary: Legacy terraform/ssh-keys.tf is a 20-line file with exactly ONE functional element: resource tls_private_key.ansible (algorithm = ED25519), which generates the SSH keypair Ansible uses to reach provisioned VMs/containers. Everything else in the file is comment documentation. The resource is carried VERBATIM into tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:28-30 (identical resource name, type, and algorithm). The provider that backs it (hashicorp/tls) is declared in tofu/opconnect/providers.tf:31-35 and locked in tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl:110. The keypair was moved into the opconnect state (Scheme B / 3-state split) and its 1Password STORAGE path was refactored per "Option D" — the legacy header noted the key "is stored in 1Password via onepassword.tf" using the onepassword TF provider; tofu now writes both halves to 1Password via a desktop op-CLI null_resource (tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:38-61, scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh) to keep opconnect Connect-independent. That storage-side refactor belongs to legacy onepassword.tf, NOT this file; the only element THIS file defines (the tls_private_key resource) is carried 1:1. No outputs, variables, data sources, locals, lifecycle blocks, meta-args, or provider blocks exist in the legacy file.

elements.name: tls_private_key.ansible
elements.detail: ED25519 SSH keypair for Ansible to SSH into provisioned VMs/containers. Legacy: resource "tls_private_key" "ansible" { algorithm = "ED25519" } at terraform/ssh-keys.tf:18-20.
elements.evidence: Legacy terraform/ssh-keys.tf:18-20 defines resource tls_private_key.ansible { algorithm = "ED25519" }. Carried VERBATIM to tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:28-30 (same resource name "ansible", same type tls_private_key, same algorithm ED25519). Provider hashicorp/tls declared at tofu/opconnect/providers.tf:31-35 and locked at tofu/opconnect/.terraform.lock.hcl:110. grep 'tls_private_key' across tofu/+modules/ returns exactly one resource definition: tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:28. Resource moved into opconnect state per 3-state split (Scheme B). Behavior equivalent: still generates the Ansible ED25519 keypair. Downstream wiring also present: public key feeds opconnect cloud-init via local ansible_ssh_public_key (tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:63-67); both halves written to 1Password via null_resource.ansible_ssh_key_writeback (lines 38-61); compute READS the public key back as a data source (tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:21-39).

elements.name: header comment block (Key Architecture / Bootstrap vs Infrastructure-Ansible key)
elements.detail: Lines 1-15: documents the two-key architecture and notes 'The key is stored in 1Password via onepassword.tf'. Non-functional documentation; the '1Password storage' it references is refactored under Option D (now op-CLI write-back in opconnect, not the onepassword provider).
elements.evidence: The legacy header's storage claim ('stored in 1Password via onepassword.tf', i.e. onepassword TF provider) is intentionally refactored per the KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE (opconnect Option D dropped the onepassword PROVIDER from opconnect, replaced with tls_private_key + null_resource op-CLI write-back via scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh). The equivalent documentation now lives in the expanded header at tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:1-26 plus the providers.tf:8,31-35 notes. No functional element is lost — only the documented storage mechanism moved. Storage resource itself belonged to legacy onepassword.tf, audited under that unit.

units.summary: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled (43 lines) is an INERT/DISABLED file in the legacy snapshot. The `.disabled` extension means Terraform never loads it — it was an EARLIER alternative SSH-key design that was superseded by the live legacy approach. It defines one data source `onepassword_item.infrastructure_ssh_key` that READS a pre-existing 1Password item titled "Infrastructure SSH Key (Terraform)" plus three outputs exposing the read key's public_key, fingerprint, and 1Password item id. The ACTIVE legacy design (terraform/ssh-keys.tf + onepassword.tf) instead GENERATES the keypair via tls_private_key.ansible and WRITES it into 1Password — confirming the .disabled file's read-only approach was the abandoned alternative. Deployed-state evidence confirms inertness: the legacy terraform.tfstate has 0 onepassword_item entries and no infrastructure_ssh keys. PARITY VERDICT: because the file was disabled/inert, it contributed nothing to the deployed estate, so there is nothing it was responsible for that could be lost. Separately, the read-via-data-source PATTERN it embodied was actually ADOPTED in the tofu migration — tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf reads the ansible key via `data onepassword_item.ansible_ssh_key` (the exact mechanism this file used), keyed on var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title rather than a hard-coded title. No parity gap. Every element is either intentionally inert (the file as a whole) and/or its functionality is present in tofu (the data-source-read mechanism and a public-key output).

elements.name: ssh-keys.tf.disabled (whole file — .disabled / inert)
elements.detail: The .disabled extension excludes this file from Terraform's load (only *.tf is parsed). It was an earlier ALTERNATIVE SSH-key design (READ a pre-existing 1Password item) that was abandoned in favor of the live legacy GENERATE-and-WRITE design in terraform/ssh-keys.tf (tls_private_key.ansible) + terraform/onepassword.tf (onepassword_item.ansible_ssh_key). It contributed nothing to the deployed estate.
elements.evidence: Filename ends in .tf.disabled (Terraform ignores non-.tf files). Legacy on-disk terraform.tfstate: `grep -c onepassword_item` = 0, no `infrastructure_ssh` keys present — never applied. Active legacy terraform/ssh-keys.tf:18 `resource tls_private_key.ansible` and terraform/onepassword.tf:21 `resource onepassword_item.ansible_ssh_key` are the design that actually shipped.
elements.drop_reason: Inert/disabled file in the snapshot — never loaded by Terraform, never in deployed state. It is the relic of a superseded design (read pre-existing key) replaced by the live generate-and-store design. Nothing to carry; carrying it would re-introduce the abandoned alternative.

elements.name: data.onepassword_item.infrastructure_ssh_key
elements.detail: Legacy (disabled) data source read a 1Password item by hardcoded title "Infrastructure SSH Key (Terraform)" in vault var.onepassword_vault_id to surface its public key. The tofu migration adopts this exact READ-via-data-source mechanism in compute state, but parameterized by a title variable and pointed at the ansible key item. Even though the legacy block was inert, its functional intent (read the SSH public key from 1Password without putting the private key in state) is present and live in tofu.
elements.evidence: Legacy disabled block: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled:24-27 `data "onepassword_item" "infrastructure_ssh_key" { vault = var.onepassword_vault_id; title = "Infrastructure SSH Key (Terraform)" }`. Tofu equivalent: tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:21-24 `data "onepassword_item" "ansible_ssh_key" { vault = var.onepassword_vault_id; title = var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title }` — same data-source type, same vault var, title now a variable. var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title declared at tofu/compute/variables.tf:38; var.onepassword_vault_id at tofu/compute/variables.tf:11.

elements.name: vault = var.onepassword_vault_id (on the disabled data source)
elements.detail: The vault argument referencing var.onepassword_vault_id is preserved verbatim in the tofu compute data source.
elements.evidence: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled:25 `vault = var.onepassword_vault_id` -> tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:22 `vault = var.onepassword_vault_id`; variable defined tofu/compute/variables.tf:11.

elements.name: title = "Infrastructure SSH Key (Terraform)" (hardcoded item title)
elements.detail: The hardcoded item-title literal is replaced by a variable (var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title) in tofu. The literal string itself does not appear anywhere in tofu — but it was a hardcoded value inside a never-applied file, so its absence is a deliberate refactor (literal -> variable), not a gap.
elements.evidence: `grep -rn 'Infrastructure SSH Key' tofu modules` returns NO hits (searched all tofu/* and modules/* — absent). Replacement: tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:23 `title = var.ansible_ssh_key_item_title`; variable at tofu/compute/variables.tf:38; same var also drives the write-side title at tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:41,53.

elements.name: output.infrastructure_ssh_public_key
elements.detail: Legacy (disabled) output exposed the read public key. Tofu compute exposes the ansible SSH public key as output `ansible_ssh_public_key` (renamed, now marked sensitive). The active legacy config already had this same output (terraform/outputs.tf:113), so the disabled variant's output is fully covered.
elements.evidence: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled:30-33 `output "infrastructure_ssh_public_key" { value = data.onepassword_item.infrastructure_ssh_key.public_key }` -> tofu/compute/outputs.tf:107-113 `output "ansible_ssh_public_key" { value = local.ansible_ssh_public_key; sensitive = true }`, where local.ansible_ssh_public_key (tofu/compute/ssh-keys.tf:36-38) is extracted from the same data source. Also present in active legacy at terraform/outputs.tf:113.

elements.name: output.infrastructure_ssh_fingerprint
elements.detail: Legacy (disabled) output exposed the SSH key fingerprint read from the 1Password item. No fingerprint OUTPUT exists in any tofu state. The fingerprint is still COMPUTED and USED inside tofu/opconnect (as the write-back trigger and an op-CLI env var), but it is not surfaced as a root output. Since this output lived in a never-applied file and the value is an internal mechanism detail (not a consumed interface), its absence as an output is immaterial to parity.
elements.evidence: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled:35-38 `output "infrastructure_ssh_fingerprint" { value = data.onepassword_item.infrastructure_ssh_key.fingerprint }`. Tofu: `grep -rn 'fingerprint' tofu/*/outputs.tf` -> NO output hits (searched tofu/compute/outputs.tf, tofu/opconnect/outputs.tf, tofu/network/outputs.tf — absent as output). Fingerprint IS used internally: tofu/opconnect/ssh-keys.tf:40 `fingerprint = tls_private_key.ansible.public_key_fingerprint_sha256` (null_resource trigger) and :57 `SSH_PUBLIC_KEY_FP`. The active legacy config did surface it (terraform/outputs.tf:120), but the source value there is the generated key, not a 1Password read.
elements.drop_reason: Output came from an inert/never-applied file; the fingerprint is now an internal mechanism (write-back trigger / op-CLI env) rather than a consumed root output. No downstream consumer depends on it, so not exposing it is not a parity loss.

elements.name: output.infrastructure_ssh_1password_id
elements.detail: Legacy (disabled) output exposed the 1Password item ID of the read SSH key. No equivalent SSH-key-item-ID output exists in tofu (tofu/compute/outputs.tf exposes IDs for the secrets it CREATES — gitlab/postgres/mealie/etc — but not the ansible SSH key item, which it only READS). This output lived in a never-applied file and exposes an item ID for a bootstrap-seeded item not owned by compute; its absence is immaterial to parity.
elements.evidence: terraform/ssh-keys.tf.disabled:40-43 `output "infrastructure_ssh_1password_id" { value = data.onepassword_item.infrastructure_ssh_key.id }`. Tofu: tofu/compute/outputs.tf:82-100 `output "onepassword_items"` lists IDs only for onepassword_item RESOURCES it creates (postgresql_admin, mealie_db_user, tandoor_db_user, gitlab_root_password, gitlab_runner_token, tandoor_secret_key, immich_db_password) — the ansible_ssh_key is a DATA source (read, not owned), so no .id output for it (searched tofu/compute/outputs.tf, tofu/opconnect/outputs.tf — absent).
elements.drop_reason: Output came from an inert/never-applied file and exposed the item ID of a bootstrap-seeded item that compute only reads (not owns). No downstream consumer; the active legacy config never exposed it either. Not a parity loss.

units.summary: All functional elements of the 61-line legacy ubuntu-template.tf were carried into tofu/network/templates.tf. Two resources (the ubuntu-template VM and the ubuntu cloud-image download_file) are present with equivalent behavior. Per Scheme B the VM was renumbered 1000->9001 (refactored), and per the greenfield storage lock the datastore moved vm-data->local-zfs on both the init drive and the OS disk (refactored). The only legacy attribute not reproduced is initialization.file_format="qcow2", correctly dropped because the template now lives on the local-zfs block backend (file_format applies to the qcow2-backed vm-data datastore it left). The template VMID is now exported via network/outputs.tf:ubuntu_template_vm_id and consumed cross-state by opconnect/compute via the proxmox-vm module's template_vm_id (default 9001). No parity gaps (no MISSING).

units.summary: Legacy terraform/variables.tf defined 6 input variables driving the flat single-state config. In the OpenTofu migration these were redistributed across the 3 states + modules. proxmox_api_url carried verbatim into all 3 states. proxmox_ssh_host was a DANGLING/unused variable in legacy (defined, never referenced — both legacy and tofu provider ssh blocks use agent=true with no host), so its absence is harmless. vm_configs (a for_each map of 5 VM specs) was refactored into per-app explicit module calls under Scheme B VMIDs; its object fields (memory_mb/cpu_cores/started/on_boot/full_clone/datastore_id) became proxmox-vm module variables. disabled_workloads survives, refactored with a tighter validation. postgresql_lxc_config (object) was refactored into the explicit module "postgresql" LXC call. The one real parity concern: postgresql_databases — the structured list of DBs with name/description/owner that ansible consumed (via the legacy databases output / postgresql_connection.databases) to create databases and users — has NO structured equivalent in tofu. DB names survive only implicitly as the `database` field on 1Password items in secrets.tf; the owner/description metadata and the ansible-consumable list are gone, and this is not covered by any KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE.

elements.name: proxmox_api_url
elements.detail: type=string, default="https://192.168.40.206:8006/", description="Proxmox VE API URL". Legacy variables.tf:1-5. Consumed by the proxmox provider endpoint.
elements.evidence: Present identically in all 3 split states: tofu/network/variables.tf:5-9, tofu/opconnect/variables.tf:5-9, tofu/compute/variables.tf:5-9 (same default "https://192.168.40.206:8006/"). Consumed at tofu/{network,opconnect,compute}/providers.tf endpoint=var.proxmox_api_url (network:25, opconnect:46, compute:35).

elements.name: proxmox_ssh_host
elements.detail: type=string, default="192.168.40.206", description="Proxmox VE SSH host for template management and remote operations". Legacy variables.tf:7-11. DANGLING: defined but NEVER referenced anywhere in the legacy tree — the legacy provider ssh block (provider.tf:36-40) uses agent=true/username=terraform with NO host argument.
elements.evidence: grep proxmox_ssh_host across entire legacy tree = 1 hit, the definition only (variables.tf:7); zero usages. grep proxmox_ssh_host across entire tofu/+modules/ tree = 0 hits (absent). Both legacy provider.tf:36-40 and tofu/{network,opconnect,compute}/providers.tf ssh blocks are functionally identical: { agent=true, username="terraform" } with no host arg (SSH_AUTH_SOCK used). Dropping an unused variable changes no behavior.
elements.drop_reason: Dead/unused variable in legacy — defined in variables.tf but never wired to any resource/provider (provider ssh block used agent=true with no host). Not a functional element; tofu provider ssh blocks replicate the same hostless agent config, so no behavior lost.

elements.name: vm_configs
elements.detail: map(object({vm_id,name,memory_mb,cpu_cores,started, on_boot=opt(true), full_clone=opt(true), datastore_id=opt("vm-data")})) with 5 default entries: gitlab(1103,16384,8), mealie(1104,2048,2), tandoor(1105,2048,2), immich(1106,8192,4,local-zfs), runitup(1111,4096,4). Legacy variables.tf:13-64. Drove the qemu-vm + cloud-init for_each, dns.tf fcx_vms, and ansible-inventory.
elements.evidence: The single for_each map was replaced by explicit per-app module calls under Scheme B VMIDs: gitlab (tofu/compute/gitlab.tf:13-29, vm_id=1201, cores=8, memory_mb=16384), mealie (mealie.tf, 1301), tandoor (tandoor.tf, 1302), immich (immich.tf, 1303), runitup (runitup.tf, 1304). The object fields became proxmox-vm module variables: vm_id/name/cores/memory_mb/datastore_id (modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:1-25), started (variables.tf:49-52 -> main.tf:34,105), on_boot (variables.tf:55 -> main.tf:35,106). full_clone is implicit in the module's clone block. Mapped VMIDs match the KNOWN INTENTIONAL Scheme B (gitlab 1201, mealie 1301, tandoor 1302, immich 1303, runitup 1304).
elements.tofu_location: tofu/compute/gitlab.tf:13; tofu/compute/mealie.tf; tofu/compute/tandoor.tf; tofu/compute/immich.tf; tofu/compute/runitup.tf; modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf:1-55

elements.detail: optional(bool, true) per-VM field selecting full vs linked clone. Legacy variables.tf:21.
elements.evidence: No explicit full_clone variable in modules/proxmox-vm/variables.tf (searched: vm_id/name/cores/memory_mb/datastore_id/template_vm_id/user_data_file_id/vendor_data_file_id/tags/started/on_boot/extra_disk_size_gb/protected — no full_clone). The proxmox-vm module clones from template_vm_id; whether it sets full_clone (default true behavior) is in modules/proxmox-vm/main.tf clone block (not read in full here). Behavior likely preserved by the bpg provider default (full clone) but the explicit knob is not surfaced as a module var.

elements.name: disabled_workloads
elements.detail: type=list(string), default=[], dev escape-hatch listing vm_configs keys to EXCLUDE. Legacy validation: every entry must be a key of vm_configs (variables.tf:75-84). Fed local.enabled_vm_configs (qemu-vm/cloud-init for_each, dns fcx_vms, ansible-inventory, try()-guarded outputs).
elements.evidence: Survives in tofu/compute/variables.tf:27-36 (same name/type/default), but validation TIGHTENED: condition now restricts entries to disposable services only — alltrue([for w: contains(["mealie","tandoor","immich","runitup"], w)]) (variables.tf:33-34), because gitlab/postgresql are now protected singletons (always built, no count guard). Gating mechanism changed from for_each filter to per-file count = contains(var.disabled_workloads,"<svc>") ? 0 : 1 (mealie.tf:12,30,38; tandoor.tf:10,28,36; immich.tf:13,31,39; runitup.tf:13,31,39) and conditional inventory merges (ansible-inventory.tf:46,52,58,64). NOTE: this contradicts the KNOWN-INTENTIONAL phrasing that 'disabled_workloads handling' was removed with the backup stack — the variable was NOT removed, it was carried+refactored.

Object fields became proxmox-lxc module vars: vm_id/hostname/cores/memory_mb/disk_gb/tags (modules/proxmox-lxc/variables.tf:1-45), started (variables.tf:48 -> main.tf:8), on_boot (variables.tf:54 -> main.tf:9 start_on_boot). Per its own comment, ansible consumed this to CREATE databases and users; exposed via legacy outputs.tf databases map (lines 42-48) and postgresql_connection.databases (line 77).

Searched tofu+modules for owner/db.name/database-owner/create-database/db_user: only hits are 1Password item resources onepassword_item.mealie_db_user (secrets.tf:56) / tandoor_db_user (secrets.tf:75) and their IDs in outputs.tf:91-92 — these carry credentials, not a name/description/owner provisioning list. tofu/compute/outputs.tf postgresql_connection (lines 69-76) DROPPED the `databases` member the legacy output had (legacy outputs.tf:77). The DB NAMES survive implicitly as the `database` field on 1Password items (secrets.tf:47 postgres, :66 mealie, :85 tandoor), but the owner/description metadata and the ansible-consumable database list are gone. NOT covered by any KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE (backup-stack removal doesn't touch this). Flagged uncertain (not hard-MISSING) because DB creation may now be hardcoded in the ansible role rather than driven by a TF-emitted list — that role is outside the audited tofu/ tree and cannot be confirmed here.

units.summary: The assigned authoritative artifact (ondisk/terraform.tfstate) is an EMPTY DRAINED STUB — serial 2654 proves it once held the full deployment, but resources:[] and outputs:{} now (322 bytes). It provides NO usable deployed-resource list (state migrated to the new remote S3+SSE-KMS backend, or destroyed; only surviving trace is a check_results entry for the intentionally-dropped var.disabled_workloads). I reconstructed the deployed inventory from the legacy SOURCE tree AND the LIVE new state (`tofu state list` succeeded for all 3 states). Result: every legacy-deployed resource has a semantically-equivalent resource in the new tofu tree — all carried or refactored (flat resources -> modules, split across network/opconnect/compute, VMID Scheme B). ZERO real parity gaps (no MISSING). units.summary: The two as-built tfvars files contain exactly two keys. (1) onepassword_vault_id is commented out in terraform.auto.tfvars (no literal value applied — supplied via TF_VAR_ env), and the variable is fully carried into both tofu/compute and tofu/opconnect. (2) disabled_workloads = ["gitlab"] is the only LIVE as-built value, and it is the parity gap: the variable name carried over but tofu's NEW validation explicitly rejects "gitlab" (now a protected singleton with no count guard and no independent disable toggle). The exact as-built value would now FAIL `tofu plan`, and the dev behavior it bought (skip GitLab's ~18min clone for non-SCM dev cycles) has no equivalent knob in tofu. This is a semantic change to an as-built value not covered by the stated KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGES, so it is flagged MISSING.

elements.name: disabled_workloads
elements.detail: As-built value: disabled_workloads = ["gitlab"] (the ONLY live value in dev.auto.tfvars; terraform.tfstate serial 2654 confirms var.disabled_workloads is the sole check-result object). In the legacy tree gitlab was a first-class gateable key — present in var.vm_configs (gitlab vm_id 1103, legacy variables.tf:26-32) and the legacy validation (variables.tf:80-83) accepted ANY vm_configs key, so ["gitlab"] was VALID and HONORED: it excluded GitLab from the build to skip its ~18min clone during non-SCM dev cycles (dev.auto.tfvars header lines 14-17). The tofu variable EXISTS (tofu/compute/variables.tf:27-36) and the gating wiring exists for disposable services, BUT the tofu validation (tofu/compute/variables.tf:33-34) restricts the list to exactly [mealie, tandoor, immich, runitup] and EXPLICITLY rejects gitlab: error_message says "gitlab and postgresql are protected singletons and cannot be disabled." gitlab is now protected=true / always-built with NO count guard and NO independent disable toggle (tofu/compute/gitlab.tf:4-9,22). Net effect: the exact as-built value ["gitlab"] would now FAIL `tofu plan` validation, and there is NO knob in tofu to reproduce the as-built dev behavior of skipping the GitLab build. The KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGES (backup-stack kill, S3 backend, encryption, 3-state split, VMID Scheme B, opconnect Option D) do NOT mention removing gitlab's dev-skip toggle, so this is an unjustified parity gap.

confirmed_gaps.name: runcmd: DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive dpkg-reconfigure postfix
confirmed_gaps.detail: HIGHEST-IMPACT LOSS. The debconf_selections block is carried (preseeds postfix answers), but the runcmd that actually applies them non-interactively (dpkg-reconfigure postfix) is dropped. postfix is installed and preseeded but never reconfigured at boot, so the Internet Site / mailname config may not take effect as it did in legacy. No KNOWN INTENTIONAL CHANGE justifies this.
confirmed_gaps.evidence: CONFIRMED ABSENT, NOT REFACTORED. (1) `grep -rn 'dpkg-reconfigure'` across the entire repo (tofu/, modules/, ansible/, scripts/) returns ZERO hits. (2) modules/cloud-init/main.tf:14-24 builds vendor_data_config with `runcmd = var.runcmd` verbatim — there is NO auto-injection of a reconfigure command when `debconf_selections` is set; the module renders only whatever runcmd the caller passes. (3) tofu/compute/gitlab.tf (lines 31-56) passes NO runcmd override (grep 'runcmd' on the file = empty), so the module DEFAULT applies — modules/cloud-init/variables.tf:37-40 = only `systemctl enable/start qemu-guest-agent`. (4) The `debconf_selections` block IS carried (gitlab.tf:52-55), confirming the reviewer's exact claim: postfix is preseeded but the command that re-applies it non-interactively at boot is dropped. (5) The ansible gitlab role installs postfix (ansible/roles/gitlab/tasks/install.yml:13) but does NOT reconfigure it — no debconf/dpkg-reconfigure task anywhere in the role, so the functionality was NOT moved into Ansible. (6) Legacy file recovered from git (`git show 5baffbb~1:terraform/cloud-init-gitlab.tf`) confirms the legacy runcmd had `- DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive dpkg-reconfigure postfix` plus `systemctl enable/start ssh`, smoke tests, and marker files — ALL of which are gone, not just the one line. NOT an intentional design removal: docs/enhance-harden-later.md:91 explicitly tracks this as deferred backlog item #65 "gitlab cloud-init runcmd parity (postfix + smoke tests + marker)" — a known parity gap to be fixed later, the opposite of the deliberately-killed backup stack. FUNCTIONAL RELEVANCE: GitLab is configured to send mail via local postfix (ansible/roles/gitlab/templates/gitlab.rb.j2:67-70: smtp_enable=true, smtp_address=localhost, smtp_port=25), so postfix being correctly set as Internet Site with the right mailname matters for outbound email. Severity is IMPORTANT not CRITICAL because the debconf preseed is still present and cloud-init's debconf-set-selections stage normally runs before the postfix package install, so postfix's postinst typically self-configures from the preseed at first boot; the dropped dpkg-reconfigure was the legacy author's re-application safety net against preseed-ordering flakiness. It does not break the deploy, lose a deployed resource, or lose a secret.
confirmed_gaps.remediation:
Add an explicit runcmd override to module "gitlab_cloud_init" in tofu/compute/gitlab.tf so the postfix preseed is force-applied at boot (and restore the dropped ssh-enable for parity). Append after the debconf_selections block (line 55):

  runcmd = [
    "systemctl enable qemu-guest-agent",
    "systemctl start qemu-guest-agent",
    "systemctl enable ssh",
    "systemctl start ssh",
    "DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive dpkg-reconfigure postfix",
  ]

(The module default only enables qemu-guest-agent, so the override must re-list those two lines to avoid regressing them.) Note: the smoke-test verification block and the /var/lib/cloud-init.gitlab.ready marker were also dropped as part of backlog item #65 — if full parity is wanted, add those runcmd entries too. This is tracked as #65 in docs/enhance-harden-later.md; closing this gap there is the clean path.

Exhaustive tofu absence proof:
- Enumerated ALL outputs across tofu+modules: 7 output.tf files, 28 outputs. NONE named ansible_ssh_key_fingerprint; NONE reference public_key_fingerprint_sha256 (grep -A4 '^output' over every output file = zero fingerprint hits).
