- compute outputs.tf includes the SIBLING output "ansible_ssh_public_key" (line 107) but NOT the fingerprint.

blind_spots: WRONG-TREE PREMISE (biggest blind spot). The audit's unit list is the legacy FLAT `terraform/` tree (dns.tf, qemu-vm.tf, lxc-postgresql.tf, provider.tf, etc.). The tofu side is a 3-STATE restructure: tofu/network (foundation: unifi data lookup + templates), tofu/opconnect (secrets-root: SSH keypair via op CLI), tofu/compute (services via ../modules/). Many tofu files have NO legacy counterpart and were therefore NEVER in scope: encryption.tf x3, remote-state.tf x2, network.tf, templates.tf, secrets.tf, opconnect.tf, and the entire modules/ tree (cloud-init, proxmox-vm, proxmox-lxc, unifi-host). A per-LEGACY-unit audit cannot detect a gap in a file that only exists on the tofu side. The 4 confirmed gaps are all legacy->tofu line-level diffs; the harder question 'is every NEW tofu file correct' was never asked.

THE LEGACY TREE IS ALREADY DELETED FROM HEAD. Commit 5baffbb ('chore(p6): delete legacy terraform/ flat tree') already removed all .tf files. On disk, terraform/ has ZERO .tf files (only tfvars + tfstate backups) and is fully gitignored. The audit was run against GIT HISTORY (commit 39cc358), not the working tree — an unstated assumption. The framing 'safety net before the legacy tree is permanently deleted' is partly moot: HEAD already deleted it; only on-disk forensic backups remain.

MIGRATION STRATEGY = GREENFIELD DESTROY+RECREATE, NOT STATE IMPORT (this resolves the secret-regeneration risk but the audit never stated it). The live terraform/terraform.tfstate is EMPTY (serial 2654, 0 resources) — the legacy fleet was cleanly `terraform destroy`'d. P5 plan line 27 confirms: 'Legacy tree has no prevent_destroy -> clean teardown.' So the 8 onepassword_item resources in compute/secrets.tf with password_recipe generate BRAND-NEW passwords by design, and the rebuilt fleet uses them. This is only safe if teardown was complete AND ansible re-applied the new creds to every running service. The DEPLOYED-STATE-PARITY dimension nominally covers this but it is the single highest-stakes emergent behavior and deserved an explicit callout.

THE CONFIRMED 'postfix runcmd' GAP IS BIGGER THAN ONE LINE. tofu/compute/gitlab.tf's cloud-init call passes debconf_selections but passes NO `runcmd` arg at all, so the module default runcmd (only qemu-guest-agent enable/start) applies. That means the legacy gitlab runcmd block ALSO dropped: `systemctl enable/start ssh` AND the entire cloud-init smoke-test verification block (writes /var/log/cloud-init-verify.log checking curl/python3/postfix/ufw). The postfix dpkg-reconfigure is arguably redundant (debconf is pre-seeded before install), but the dropped ssh-enable and verification log were NOT flagged.

ANSIBLE INVENTORY CONTRACT CHANGED in two ways a cosmetic rating misses: (1) ansible_host failure value changed from truthy string 'IP not available' (legacy) to NULL (tofu, intentional, lets Jinja default() engage) — any playbook/template testing for the literal string silently breaks; (2) postgresql host `name` changed from computed initialization[0].hostname to static literal 'postgresql'. Both are real downstream contract changes for the cloud.terraform dynamic inventory consumed by ansible/inventory/terraform.yml (project_path -> ../tofu/compute).

1PASSWORD / SECRETS ARCHITECTURE FUNDAMENTALLY CHANGED across states. Legacy onepassword.tf created 9 items incl. the ansible SSH key via the onepassword PROVIDER. Tofu splits this: opconnect deliberately DROPS the onepassword provider and writes the SSH keypair via the desktop `op` CLI (scripts/op-write-ssh-key.sh, Connect-free bootstrap), while compute/secrets.tf recreates the other 8 items via the provider AND reads the SSH key as a data source (ansible_ssh_key_item_title, bootstrap-seeded, private key never in state). This 3-way secrets handoff (opconnect writes key -> compute reads key -> ansible consumes by title) is pure emergent cross-state behavior invisible to any single-file audit.

PROVIDER/VERSION DRIFT not in scope. required_version relaxed >=1.9 (legacy, needed for cross-variable validation referencing keys(var.vm_configs)) to >=1.8 (all 3 tofu states). Safe ONLY because the validation was rewritten to a hardcoded literal list ['mealie','tandoor','immich','runitup'] instead of referencing another variable — but that list is now a maintenance-coupled magic constant that can silently drift from the actual service set. Also: opconnect added hashicorp/null 3.3.0 (for the op-CLI write-back null_resource) — a new provider with no legacy analog.

ENCRYPTION + REMOTE-STATE DECRYPT WIRING is the subtlest emergent dependency. All 3 states use AES-GCM state encryption via aws_kms key_provider 'state'. opconnect + compute additionally need a `remote_state_data_sources { default { method } }` block to DECRYPT the network state they read via terraform_remote_state — without it the cross-state read fails 'Unsupported state file format'. network/ has NO such block (it reads nothing). This asymmetry is correct but is exactly the kind of thing a per-file audit rates as boilerplate and skips; if the network state's encryption ever diverges from the consumers' default decryptor, all cross-state reads break.

DNS BEHAVIOR MOVED, NOT COPIED. Legacy terraform/dns.tf (a named unit) had no 1:1 tofu file — the unifi_client reservation + A-record logic moved into modules/unifi-host, invoked per-service in compute (gitlab_dns, etc.). It faithfully reproduces the observe-then-pin/lower(mac)/network_id-omit behavior AND hardens it (nullable=false fails loud at module boundary vs legacy silent try()). This is fine, but the audit pairing 'terraform/dns.tf' against nothing on the tofu side risks rating the whole DNS flow as a deletion rather than a relocation.
